qianyi_wang.name: Qianyi Wang
qianyi_wang.summary:
Qianyi Wang is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U15 level, with 6 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Qianyi is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
qianyi_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Qianyi Wang, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 6 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Qianyi is right on schedule. It all started at 2021 HPBC NW ORC, and so far, the only thing Qianyi has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Qianyi saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Qianyi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 6 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Qianyi has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Qianyi has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Qianyi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15, which means Qianyi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Qianyi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Qianyi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Qianyi is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Qianyi wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Qianyi, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

qingyao_liu.name: Qingyao Liu
qingyao_liu.summary:
A California competitor, Qingyao Liu is a Regional Championship titlist, currently competing at the U13 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

A true triple-threat, Qingyao competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • 2 ORC titles.

With 19 different doubles partners over the years, Qingyao adapts to anyone:
  • Hannah Xiao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Aayush Jaladurgam (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Reyansh Chalamalasetti (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Xiner Hu (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #4)

Qingyao has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Qingyao has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Qingyao is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
qingyao_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Qingyao Liu, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 56 career entries in 19 tournaments, Qingyao has 2 titles — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 27 entries where Qingyao drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 56 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Qingyao saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Qingyao's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Qingyao actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Qingyao is 0-for-15. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Qingyao would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Qingyao has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Qingyao has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Qingyao's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Qingyao plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Qingyao is 0-for-18. Zero titles in 18 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Qingyao and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Qingyao needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Qingyao has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Qingyao's texts. The go-to partner is Regina Lee (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11 — we hope Aiden Yanyi Tan has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Qingyao entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Qingyao's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Qingyao and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Qingyao has shown up 9 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Qingyao is not just losing locally — Qingyao is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Liu family travels cross-country so Qingyao can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Qingyao has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Qingyao showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U11, which means Qingyao traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event, where Qingyao entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Qingyao paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 56 entries, and 54 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Qingyao looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Qingyao's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Qingyao has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Qingyao. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

qinxian_zhu.name: Qinxian Zhu
qinxian_zhu.summary:
Representing California, Qinxian Zhu has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 13 entries across 6 tournaments, Qinxian is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

What makes Qinxian especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Qinxian has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Jonathan Yung-Nien Chuang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #3)
  • Luna Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events)

Qinxian has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Qinxian's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
qinxian_zhu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Qinxian Zhu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 13 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 13-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Qinxian's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Qinxian keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Qinxian saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Qinxian plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 13 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Qinxian has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Qinxian showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U13, which means Qinxian traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Qinxian entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Qinxian paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Qinxian keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Qinxian has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Qinxian, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Qinxian. We will be here, taking notes.

quintus_zhang.name: Quintus Zhang
quintus_zhang.summary:
Quintus Zhang is just getting started on the junior circuit with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Quintus's.

Quintus competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
